title: The Christian Child
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Christian Child - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 6_1-4.txt
outline: Context: the 45th message in the Ephesians series
Baptism of Julius — God’s providence in the passage chosen
Redemption makes us new creatures (2 Corinthians 5:17)
Command to children: obey your parents in the Lord (v.1-2)
The fifth commandment: the first commandment with a promise
Command to fathers: do not provoke your children to wrath (v.4)
Bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord
summary: The 45th sermon in an expository series on Ephesians, preached on the occasion of a baptism. Exposition of Ephesians 6:1-4 covers the Christian child’s call to obey parents in the Lord (the fifth commandment, the first with a promise of blessing), and the Christian father’s responsibility to raise children in the training and admonition of the Lord without provoking them to wrath.
transcript:
For those visiting with us, this is the 45th message on the book of Ephesians — we go through books of the Bible verse by verse. In God's providence, we fall on this passage on the occasion of the baptism of Julius.

## Christ Makes the Family New

When God redeemed us through the person and work of Jesus Christ, He made us new creatures. Paul says in 2 Corinthians 5 that if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation — all things have passed away, behold, all things have become new. As new creatures who are recreated in the image of Jesus Christ, we now sit in heavenly places with Him (Ephesians 2:6). We have been enabled by God's grace to live heavenly lives here and now. That includes our family life.

Because of Christ, the wife submits to the husband and the husband loves the wife. Because of Christ, the child obeys the parents and the parents do not discourage their children. This is gospel living — it is because of the grace of God in Christ that we are able to do these things.

We cannot do justice to Paul's teaching in Ephesians 6 without bringing together the parallel passage in Colossians 3. When we put Colossians 3 and Ephesians 6 together, we see several things that mark the parent-child relationship:

- The glory of God is the goal of parenting and being parented — as Paul says in Colossians, it pleases God.
- Gospel obedience has no other limitation than the Word of God — Paul says we do this "in the Lord."
- Parenting methods and styles can easily become a source of discouragement to children — Paul exhorts fathers not to provoke them.
- Fathers may naturally tend to provoke their children and need to be aware of that.
- The basis for obedience is the moral law — Paul quotes the fifth commandment and is not afraid of telling people we must obey the law.
- Fathers must play an active and central role in bringing up their children. The idea that the father goes to work while the mother does all the child-rearing is not what the Bible teaches.
- There are benefits in obeying God in this area — Paul says this is the commandment with a promise.

## Baptized Children Are Members of the Church

I want to start by noticing that Paul addresses the children as part of the church. If we go back to chapter 1, verse 2, Paul says he is writing to the saints in Ephesus. And yet in chapter 6 he directs his words to the children, which makes them part of the saints — members of the church in Ephesus. A baptized child of a member of the church is also a member of the church and should be addressed as such.

Children, if you are baptized, you are members of the church. You are not going to become members later on. It is true that there is a status change in membership when children make a public profession of faith. But the change is not from non-member to member, but from covenant member to communicant member — one now able to partake of the Lord's Supper.

It is because baptized children of believers are members of the body of Christ that Paul can call them to obedience. If they are not part of the church of Jesus Christ, Paul has no right to call them to obedience.

The implication for parents is that we treat and raise our children as Christians. We keep on pressing the gospel upon them as those who belong to the Lord. As I've heard a pastor say: we turn to our children and say, "Mom and I are going to heaven, and you are coming with us." We keep on pressing the gospel upon them as ones who belong to the Lord.

This idea might sound strange or even heretical, and yet you practice it even unwittingly. How many of you have taught your children to pray? The Bible teaches that God does not listen to the prayers of the heathen. Do you stop teaching your children to pray because God is not their God? Ephesians 6:1 — "obey your parents in the Lord" — is among the first passages children are told to memorize. Yet if they do not belong to the Lord, you have no claim to call them to obey you in the Lord. We do not tell our children, "God is not your Father — don't believe in Him as your Father." We act on the innate belief that our children belong to the Lord.

It is because our children do belong to the Lord that the task of raising them is so important.

> *"Behold, children are a heritage from the LORD, the fruit of the womb a reward."* (Psalm 127:3)

I share the following because it is so striking I keep returning to it — a letter written December 25, 1825 by Charles Hodge, then a professor at Princeton Seminary, on the occasion of his first daughter's baptism. He writes to his mother about how Dr. Alexander conducted the service and baptized little Mary Elizabeth Hodge. He says:

*"I never appreciated so highly before the privilege of thus giving to God what is dearest to us on earth. We feel now as though she were not our own, but something lent to be cultivated and prepared through our agency for heaven. To be instrumental in thus training up one of the children of the Lord, to be presented before Him without spot or blemish, is so delightful and honorable."*

That is what we are doing when we raise our children in the Lord. The fact that our children are in a special relationship with the Lord should motivate us to be faithful in raising them. It should actually encourage us to keep the gospel before them at every opportunity, because God has promised to be their God. Therefore, we keep on putting their God before them, that they may come to their own faith.

## A Word to the Children

Now I want to speak directly to the children for a few minutes. This is simple. God wants you to obey and honor your parents.

**Kids, you need your parents.** That is how God made you. God designed it that way so that you can grow up and mature under their care. The Bible says, "Foolishness is bound up in the heart of a child, but the rod of correction will drive it far from him" (Proverbs 22:15), and "the rod and rebuke give wisdom, but a child left to himself brings shame to his mother."

God put you in the family you are in. If that is a Christian family, the promise is that God will work for good for those who love Him and are called according to His purpose.

In our culture, our phones have become the biggest influence in our lives. That is not how God designed your life. God designed your life so that the greatest influence in your life is your parents.

> *"My son, hear the instruction of your father, and do not forsake the law of your mother. For they will be a graceful ornament on your head, and chains about your neck."* (Proverbs 1:8–9)

God has given your parents the right to tell you what to do. Obey them immediately and cheerfully, without complaining or challenging their authority. Philippians 2:14 says, "Do all things without complaining and disputing." Colossians 3:23 says, "Whatever you do, do it heartily, as to the Lord and not to men."

**Respect your parents.** Your parents are not your buddies, your besties, or your peers. They are your superiors, and you must treat them as such. Honor begins in your heart, and you are to honor both mom and dad — not trying to pit one against the other.

It is not only enough to speak to them with respect, but also to speak about them with respect. Leviticus 20:9 warns, "Everyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death." Proverbs 30:17 paints a dramatic picture: "The eye that mocks his father and scorns obedience to his mother, the ravens of the valley will pick it out and the young eagles will eat it."

**Listen to your parents' teaching.** There is no one on earth who cares more about you than your parents.

> *"My son, keep your father's command, and do not forsake the law of your mother. Bind them continually upon your heart; tie them around your neck. When you roam, they will lead you; when you sleep, they will keep you; and when you awake, they will speak with you."* (Proverbs 6:20–22)

Receive their discipline. Hebrews 12 says that a son or daughter who is not disciplined is not loved. The parents' willingness to do the hard work of disciplining you is a sign that they love you. Kids, be thankful for your parents and for all they do for you. Proverbs 31 says, "Her children rise up and call her blessed."

## Limits and Changes Over Time

It is true that there are limitations to your obedience. Your parents do not have the right to tell you to sin against God — you are not bound to obey when they command what is wrong. But generally you are called to obey them.

God gives you good reasons to honor your parents. Ephesians 6:1 says honor them because it is **right** — you need no more reason than that. Colossians 3:20 tells you to honor them **for the Lord's sake**. Your parents are not perfect, but your honoring them does not depend on who they are; it depends on who Christ is. The Lord Jesus Himself submitted to, honored, and obeyed sinful parents. If the perfect Son of God could do that, then so can you.

And God promises blessing to those who honor their parents.

> *"Honor your father and mother, which is the first commandment with promise: that it may be well with you and that you may live long on the earth."* (Ephesians 6:2–3)

Conversely, Proverbs 20 says that if a man curses his father and mother, his lamp will be snuffed out in darkness. Deuteronomy 21 treats persistent rebellion against parents with the utmost seriousness.

## Growing Up and the Changing Relationship

One goal of parenting is to make children ready to leave the home. Unlike marriage, the parent-child relationship must change as children grow. This ordinarily takes place when the child marries, based on Genesis 2:24. But even before marriage, the relationship between adult children and their parents is different from that of younger children.

> *"When I was a child, I spoke as a child, I understood as a child, I thought as a child; but when I became a man, I put away childish things."* (1 Corinthians 13:11)

When is a son or daughter ready to leave the home? When he or she is no longer a child — more precisely, when mature enough to make wise choices independently, able to take on the responsibilities of adult life, and especially when ready to live for others. If the reason for leaving is simply to live selfishly for themselves, they are not ready.

Even as an adult child living under your parents' roof, you are to abide by their rules — it is their house. There may come a time for parents to ask an adult child to leave if they are unwilling to honor and respect them.

Even after you leave home, you are still to honor your parents. You may not need to obey them as when you were a child, but you must still honor them. Seek their counsel — it would be foolish not to consider it. Build your friendship with them. And you are responsible to care for them in their old age. In Matthew 15, Jesus rebuked the Pharisees who used religious pledges as an excuse not to support their aging parents. As I have said before: they changed our diapers when we were little; we change their diapers when they are grown old.

## Conclusion

God has promised to work through families. He said, "I will be a God to you and to your children." The summary of this whole sermon is this: children, bring delight to your parents, because that is what God calls you to do.
